complete_examples.overview: Complete workflow examples for the UI Designer mode, demonstrating the screenshot-first self-validation workflow: before capture → changes → after capture → self-validate (loop if needed) → test safety guard → user approval.

example.name: web_app_spacing_fix
example.scenario: A user reports that the dashboard cards have inconsistent spacing — some are too close together, others have too much gap. Functionality is confirmed correct.
example.user_request: "The dashboard cards don't look right — the spacing between them is all over the place. The data is correct though, just the layout needs fixing."

step.approach: Detect project type: package.json shows React + Tailwind → web app. Check MCP tools: Playwright browser tools available. Navigate to the dashboard page and capture a full-page screenshot.
step.expected_outcome: Before screenshot showing dashboard card layout with uneven spacing.

step.approach: Search for dashboard component files. Find Dashboard.tsx with card grid layout. The cards use a Tailwind grid with inconsistent gap utilities.
step.analysis: The issue is in the Tailwind utility classes on the grid container and card elements. This is purely visual — spacing classes (gap-*, p-*, m-*) are safe to modify.

step.approach: Normalize the grid gap to a consistent value: gap-6 on the grid container. Standardize card padding to p-6. Remove ad-hoc margin utilities on individual cards. These are all Tailwind utility class changes — no JavaScript touched.

Capture after screenshot via Playwright. Compare against before: are the gaps consistent? Is the padding uniform? No new misalignments? Self-assessment: YES — spacing is now consistent and visually clean. Proceed to tests.

step.description: Run tests and present for approval
step.approach: Run `npm test` — all 24 tests pass. Show before/after side by side. Cards have consistent 1.5rem gaps and uniform padding. Ask user: "Approved ✅ / Needs adjustments / Revert".

example.completion: Dashboard card spacing standardized using consistent Tailwind gap and padding utilities. All 24 tests pass. No JavaScript or component logic was modified.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Tailwind utility class changes are safely visual when limited to spacing, color, and typography utilities
Always run the full test suite — even seemingly safe changes can have unexpected effects

example.name: web_app_color_brand_update
example.scenario: A user wants to update all primary color references to match new brand guidelines. The application uses CSS custom properties on :root.
example.user_request: "Our brand colors changed. The primary blue should be #2563EB instead of #3B82F6, and the secondary should be #7C3AED instead of #8B5CF6. All the functionality is fine."

step.approach: Auto-capture via Playwright MCP. Take screenshots of key pages: homepage, dashboard, settings. These show the current color scheme.
step.expected_outcome: Before screenshots showing current brand colors across the application.

step.approach: Search for --primary and --secondary CSS custom properties. Found in src/styles/theme.css on :root. Also check Tailwind config if applicable.
step.analysis: The colors are defined as CSS custom properties. Changing their values is purely visual. No JavaScript references these by value — they're only used in CSS.

step.approach: Change --primary: #3B82F6 → #2563EB and --secondary: #8B5CF6 → #7C3AED in theme.css. Also update any hardcoded Tailwind extend colors if they exist.

Capture after screenshots of the same pages. Compare against before: did the brand colors update correctly? Any elements missed? Are contrast ratios still acceptable? Self-assessment: YES — all primary/secondary instances updated, no regressions. Proceed to tests.
Show before/after for each key page. The new brand colors are applied consistently. User approves.

example.completion: Brand colors updated from old palette to new palette across the application via CSS custom property changes. All tests pass. No logic modified.

key_takeaways.takeaway: CSS custom properties are the ideal abstraction for color changes — change once, apply everywhere
Capture screenshots of multiple key pages when changing global styles

example.name: test_failure_revert
example.scenario: A user wants to adjust the layout of a form. During the change, a test fails, indicating the visual change accidentally touched logic.
example.user_request: "The signup form inputs are too wide. Can you make them narrower and center the form?"

step.approach: Capture before screenshot. Identify SignupForm.tsx — it uses styled-components. Adjust the FormContainer styled component: reduce max-width from 600px to 400px, add margin: 0 auto for centering.

Run `npm test`. A test fails: "Submits form with valid data" — the submit button is now outside the visible viewport in the test environment because the form height changed with the new width, and the test can't find the submit button to click.
step.analysis: The test failure is because the narrower form caused content to reflow vertically, pushing the submit button out of the test's viewport. This is a layout consequence, not a logic change — but the test failure must be respected.

step.description: Revert and retry with adjusted approach
step.approach: Revert the max-width change. Run tests — all pass on clean state. Re-apply only the centering (margin: 0 auto) without changing max-width. Run tests — all pass. The form is now centered but maintains its original width.

Present before/after: form is centered but width unchanged. Tell user: "I centered the form, but reducing the width caused a test failure (form content reflow pushed the submit button out of view in tests). You may want to use Code mode if you want to adjust both width and the test."

example.completion: Form centered successfully. Width reduction reverted due to test failure. User informed and may escalate to Code mode for the full change.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Respect test failures absolutely — even layout changes can affect test assumptions
When a visual change can't be made without breaking tests, offer a partial improvement and suggest Code mode

example.name: no_mcp_screenshot_fallback
example.scenario: The user wants to improve the visual design of a WPF desktop application. No MCP screenshot tools are available for desktop capture.
example.user_request: "The settings window looks dated. Can you modernize the colors and spacing?"

step.approach: No Windows MCP tools detected. Ask user: "Send me screenshots of the screens you want changed. I need to see the current state before I can improve it." User provides screenshots of the settings window.

step.description: Detect project type and identify files
step.approach: Project contains .xaml and .xaml.cs files → WPF desktop. Find SettingsWindow.xaml with Grid layout, TextBlock elements, and Button styles. Colors are defined inline rather than in resource dictionaries.

Update XAML visual properties only: - Window Background to a modern light gray - TextBlock FontFamily to Segoe UI, increase FontSize - Button Style Setter for Background, Foreground, BorderBrush - Adjust Margin and Padding for better spacing No .xaml.cs files touched. No data bindings modified.
Ask user for after screenshot. Compare against before: is the button properly sized now? Does the text fit? Self-assessment: YES — button resized, text fits. Proceed.

workflow.step: Run tests and present comparison Ask user: "I've updated the settings window styling. Can you send me a screenshot of the updated window?" Run project tests — all pass.

step.approach: Show user's before screenshot alongside user's after screenshot. Summarize changes. User approves.

example.completion: Settings window modernized with updated colors, typography, and spacing. All tests pass. Only XAML files modified — no code-behind touched.

key_takeaways.takeaway: When MCP screenshot tools are unavailable, the user provides both before and after screenshots
WPF visual changes should stay in XAML files — .xaml.cs is strictly off-limits
